steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Alert configs in /home/user/monitoring/alerts/ aren't getting picked up by the validator anymore — used to run `./validate_alerts.sh` and it'd check all the yaml files, now it just exits silently with no output. Didn't touch the script recently, but we did reorganize the alert files into subdirectories by team last week (platform/, infra/, app/, etc). Need the validator working again so it actually finds and checks every .yaml under alerts/, however deep they're nested. There's also some yaml files that got corrupted during the reorg — seeing complaints about "duplicate keys" from whoever runs it manually on individual files, so probably need to surface those too instead of silently skipping.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@efd2c4312528:/home/user#

Analysis: I'm starting in /home/user directory. I need to investigate the monitoring/alerts/ directory structure and examine the validate_alerts.sh script to understand why it's not picking up the reorganized alert files.
Plan: First, I'll navigate to the monitoring directory and examine its structure. Then I'll look at the validate_alerts.sh script to see how it's currently finding yaml files. I'll also check the alerts directory structure to understand the reorganization into subdirectories.

results.content:
Previous response had warnings:
WARNINGS: - Extra text detected before JSON object

root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring# ls -la
total 20
drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 06:26 .

drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 06:26 alerts

-rwxrwxrwx 1 root root  125 Apr 29 06:26 validate_alerts.sh
root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring#

steps.message:
Analysis: I'm now in the monitoring directory. I can see there's a validate_alerts.sh script, a check_yaml.py helper script, and an alerts directory. I need to examine the validator script to see how it's currently finding files, and check the alerts directory structure.
Plan: First, I'll look at the validate_alerts.sh script to understand its current implementation. Then I'll check the alerts directory structure to see the subdirectories (platform/, infra/, app/, etc.). Finally, I'll examine check_yaml.py to understand how it validates yaml files.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring# cat validate_alerts.sh
#!/bin/bash
find /home/user/monitoring/alerts -maxdepth 1 -name "*.yaml" | xargs python3 /home/user/monitoring/check_yaml.py
root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring# ls -la alerts/
total 20
drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 06:26 .
drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 06:26 ..
drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 06:26 app
drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 06:26 infra
drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 06:26 platform
root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring#

steps.message:
Analysis: Found the issue! The validate_alerts.sh script uses `find` with `-maxdepth 1`, which only searches the top level of the alerts directory. Since the files were reorganized into subdirectories (app/, infra/, platform/), the script no longer finds any yaml files and exits silently. I need to check the check_yaml.py script to understand how it validates files, then fix the validator to recursively search subdirectories.
Plan: 1. First, examine check_yaml.py to see how it validates yaml files and whether it handles duplicate keys. 2. Check what yaml files exist in the subdirectories. 3. Fix validate_alerts.sh to recursively search all subdirectories by removing the -maxdepth 1 restriction. 4. Test the fixed script.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring# cat check_yaml.py
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
import yaml

for path in sys.argv[1:]:
    try:
        with open(path) as f:
            yaml.safe_load(f)
        print(f"OK: {path}")
    except yaml.YAMLError as e:
        print(f"ERROR: {path}: {e}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"ERROR: {path}: {e}")
root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring# find alerts/ -name '*.yaml'
alerts/platform/cpu_high.yaml
alerts/platform/memory_critical.yaml
alerts/infra/disk_full.yaml
alerts/infra/network_down.yaml
alerts/app/latency_spike.yaml
alerts/app/queue_depth.yaml
alerts/app/error_rate.yaml
root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring#

steps.message:
Analysis: Good! I can see there are 7 yaml files in the subdirectories. The check_yaml.py script uses yaml.safe_load() which should detect duplicate keys. Now I need to fix the validate_alerts.sh script by removing the `-maxdepth 1` restriction so it searches recursively. Let me first test the current script to confirm it produces no output, then fix it.
Plan: 1. Run the current validate_alerts.sh to confirm it exits silently with no output. 2. Fix the script by removing `-maxdepth 1` from the find command. 3. Run the fixed script to verify it finds and validates all yaml files, including detecting any duplicate key errors.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring# ./validate_alerts.sh
root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring# echo "Exit code: $?"
Exit code: 0
root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring#

steps.message:
Analysis: Confirmed - the script exits silently with no output because find with -maxdepth 1 finds no yaml files in the top-level alerts directory (they're all in subdirectories now). Now I'll fix the script by removing the -maxdepth 1 restriction.
Plan: 1. Edit validate_alerts.sh to remove the -maxdepth 1 parameter, allowing find to search recursively through all subdirectories. 2. Run the fixed script to see if it now finds and validates all yaml files, including surfacing any duplicate key errors.

results.content:

root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring# sed -i 's/-maxdepth 1 //' validate_alerts.sh

find /home/user/monitoring/alerts -name "*.yaml" | xargs python3 /home/user/monitoring/check_yaml.py

steps.message:
Analysis: Great! The validator is now finding and checking all 7 yaml files in the subdirectories. However, the task mentioned that some yaml files got corrupted with duplicate keys during the reorganization. All files are showing as OK, which means either there are no corrupted files yet, or I need to verify if yaml.safe_load() actually detects duplicate keys. Let me test this by creating a test file with duplicate keys to ensure the validator would catch such errors.
Plan: 1. Create a test yaml file with duplicate keys to verify that the validator properly detects and reports such errors. 2. Run the validator again to confirm it catches the duplicate key error. 3. Remove the test file. This will ensure the validator is working correctly and would surface any duplicate key issues if they exist.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring# echo -e 'name: test\nvalue: 1\nname: duplicate' > alerts/test_duplicate.yaml
root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring# ./validate_alerts.sh
OK: /home/user/monitoring/alerts/platform/cpu_high.yaml
OK: /home/user/monitoring/alerts/platform/memory_critical.yaml
OK: /home/user/monitoring/alerts/infra/disk_full.yaml
OK: /home/user/monitoring/alerts/infra/network_down.yaml
OK: /home/user/monitoring/alerts/app/latency_spike.yaml
OK: /home/user/monitoring/alerts/app/queue_depth.yaml
OK: /home/user/monitoring/alerts/app/error_rate.yaml
OK: /home/user/monitoring/alerts/test_duplicate.yaml
root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring#

steps.message:
Analysis: The test shows that yaml.safe_load() doesn't detect duplicate keys by default - it just silently overwrites the first value with the second. This is a known behavior of PyYAML. The task mentions that people running the validator manually on individual files are seeing 'duplicate keys' complaints, which means we need to enhance the check_yaml.py script to explicitly detect duplicate keys.
Plan: 1. Update check_yaml.py to detect duplicate keys by using a custom YAML constructor that raises an error when duplicate keys are found. 2. Test with the duplicate key file to ensure it's now detected. 3. Clean up the test file. 4. Run the final validator to confirm all real alert files are validated.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

root@efd2c4312528:/home/user/monitoring# cat > check_yaml.py << 'EOF'
> #!/usr/bin/env python3
> import sys
> import yaml
>
> def check_duplicate_keys(node, seen=None):
>     if seen is None:
>         seen = set()
>     if isinstance(node, yaml.MappingNode):
>         for key_node, value_node in node.value:
>             key = key_node.value
>             if key in seen:
>                 raise yaml.constructor.ConstructorError(
>                     "while constructing a mapping",
>                     node.start_mark,
>                     f"found duplicate key: {key}",
>                     key_node.start_mark
>                 )
>             seen.add(key)
>             check_duplicate_keys(value_node, set())
>     elif isinstance(node, yaml.SequenceNode):
>         for item in node.value:
>             check_duplicate_keys(item, set())
>
> for path in sys.argv[1:]:
>     try:
>         with open(path) as f:
>             content = f.read()
>             node = yaml.compose(content)
>             if node:
>                 check_duplicate_keys(node)
>             yaml.safe_load(content)
>         print(f"OK: {path}")
>     except yaml.YAMLError as e:
>         print(f"ERROR: {path}: {e}")
>     except Exception as e:
>         print(f"ERROR: {path}: {e}")
> EOF

steps.message:
Analysis: Excellent! The enhanced validator is now working correctly. It found the corrupted files that were mentioned in the task:
1. alerts/infra/disk_full.yaml - has duplicate 'threshold' key
2. alerts/app/error_rate.yaml - has duplicate 'severity' key
3. alerts/test_duplicate.yaml - our test file with duplicate 'name' key

The validator now recursively searches all subdirectories and properly detects duplicate keys. I should remove the test file and verify the final state.
Plan: 1. Remove the test file I created. 2. Run the validator one final time to show it's working correctly on the actual alert files. 3. Optionally show the structure to confirm everything is in order.

steps.message:
Analysis: The validator is now fully functional:
1. ✅ It recursively searches all subdirectories (platform/, infra/, app/) under alerts/
2. ✅ It finds and validates all .yaml files regardless of nesting depth
3. ✅ It detects and reports duplicate keys in YAML files
4. ✅ It surfaces the 2 corrupted files that were created during the reorganization

The fixes made:
- Modified validate_alerts.sh: Removed `-maxdepth 1` from the find command to enable recursive search
- Enhanced check_yaml.py: Added duplicate key detection using yaml.compose() and a custom checker function

The validator now properly reports errors for corrupted files and validates clean files successfully.
Plan: The task is complete. The validator script now works as required - it finds all yaml files in subdirectories and detects duplicate keys. The two corrupted files have been identified and can be fixed by the team.
